translation.content: Paulina Rodriguez, AKA Paurro, came to club music relatively late in life. It took hold for the first time when she was in her 20s, during a chance visit to the legendary London institution fabric in 2008. One look at her CV today would confirm that she's definitely made up for the late start. 16 years later, Rodriguez has worn nearly every hat in the industry. She's worked as a radio programmer at Mexico City's Aire Libre, a music PR professional and held residencies at two of Mexico City's finest clubs, YuYu and Departamento. Nowadays, she's just as hard to pin down as ever, with a global reach: she currently holds a residency with Munich's Radio 80000 and tours extensively across Europe, Asia and the Americas. The House of Paurro, her party series, is much like its globetrotting founder: this year alone there have been events at Tresor in Berlin, Making Time in Philadelphia and public records in New York. Oh yeah and don't forget, she's also producing absolute bangers, such as 2022's Galavision, in the moments in between. At the heart of it all, she's sharing unabashed joy, wherever that may be. As the saying goes, the hardest struggles often bring the greatest rewards—and Rodriguez knows this better than most. She's openly shared the challenges she faced on her way to success, from the struggles of getting bookings as a Mexican artist to more critical issues like sexual harassment and intimidation. Today she stands as an ambassador not only for her home city, but for a vision for a compassionate, community-orientated global club culture. That optimism and ambition translate into RA.966. A typical Paurro set finds its centre of gravity in house music, but from there, it's truly a case of anything goes. Her RA Podcast is no different—it's a joyride through the House of Paurro, weaving together influences spanning a rich fusion of UK, Latin and truly borderless sounds. – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I just finished my very first tour through Asia which was amazing and a dream come true. I'm also in the process of launching my own label so I'm planning everything for the first compilation which will be the first release. I also have a couple of EPs coming out next year so I'm adding the final touches to the tracks. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This mix was recorded as I toured through Asia. It was great because I felt my mind was in a very receptive and open state to catch ideas, textures and sounds—in discovery mode, in many ways. It was also very helpful because I did a few versions of it and as I was exploring a city or town I would listen to them during the day and take notes to then go back and keep working on it. While touring you spend a lot of time on your own and I really enjoy my solitude because it also allows me to have conversations with myself about creative stuff and untangle some things. It has taken me a while to break free from the box I was placed in, but I feel that I have finally reached a point where I no longer pay attention to genres. I want to be an artist who can play everything, yet I want every set to feel very me. This was the premise for this mix: to show all my different personalities beyond genre, focusing on tracks that best represent my sound. These include freestyle breaks, dreamy pads, aquatic and space-like sounds, '90s house synths, fat basslines, dubby vocals and my idol Selena. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Definitely my next releases. They're coming out on labels that I really admire and I feel very grateful that they considered me to do this. So I can't wait for them to come out. I'm really excited about the label. I don't feel like I’m reinventing the wheel here because there are some amazing labels in Latin America. However, everything I have learned at Cómeme has given me a strong perspective on how I want to approach things and I'd like to try it out. I don't want artists to simply send me their tracks for release; I want to accompany them and help them develop as artists beyond just a single release. I also want to focus on FLINTA artists in America because I sense a general fear surrounding the release of music, especially in Mexico and Latin America. Many don't believe they have what it takes, and often don't see many allies in a male-dominated scene. While it won't be exclusive, there always needs to be more of this, as in everything else I do. I'm also very excited about how things are developing for my party series, House Of Paurro, in 2025. The response from everyone has been amazing and last year it really came together as a project so now I feel it's time to take it one step further. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Obviously the Palestinian cause, which should be in everyone's heart and actions, but also one that is very concerning to me is the femicides in Mexico. Ten women are killed a day in my country and that number hasn't changed in years so it's urgent that something is done about it. What were some of the challenges you initially faced when starting your DJ career? It was extremely hard for me to be taken seriously when I started DJing. I faced serious bullying from men in the scene who were relevant at that time because I was considered "extra," partied too hard and was overweight. There was even a meme about me circulating on their social media and being shared over text (I was never able to see this meme, but I knew what it was about). I even experienced sexual abuse from a few of them. However, everything that happened helped me build my character and commit fully to my craft. I also became more aware of the people around me, which allowed me to identify who my true friends were. Eventually, I stayed with them and met more amazing people who really supported me, and without them, I wouldn't be where I am today. If you could change one thing about Mexico's nightlife scene, what would it be? I feel that Mexico's scene has a huge lack of infrastructure that doesn't let it blossom the way it should. It's extremely hard to do parties or run clubs, also the situation with the cartels is getting more delicate and that makes it unsustainable to develop projects that can last long and thrive. I also see a lot of hypocrisy and ego in the scene unfortunately. It seems like sometimes we're all fighting so no one really succeeds and that makes us even weaker towards adversity. Things are hard enough already. You started producing after several years of DJing. What prompted you to enter the studio? Do you think it's important for DJs to be producing? I took production classes around four years after DJing, but that was when I still had an office job, so I would make every excuse to not release music. However, it was more insecurity than lack of time—I was like that until the pandemic hit and suddenly I had no gigs and all the time in the world. To be honest, making music is the one thing that kept me from going crazy and not going into depression. Now, it's something I need in my life and I feel that journey has just started. I believe that you can be 100 percent successful by only playing records. However, if you feel the urge of creating and discovering your own sound out of your guts I think it's something that you have to allow yourself to do and make the effort. You don't have to release it but it really makes you know another side of you. I really feel I am more myself since I started making music.
translation.blurb: One of Mexico City's key players in session.
